questions.text: A closely wound coil of 100 turns and an area of cross-section 1 cm3 has a coefficient of self-induction 1 mH. The magnetic induction in the centre of the core of the coil when a current of 2A flows in it, will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer, derived from the formula for magnetic induction in a coil. The magnetic induction is calculated using the formula B = (μ₀ * N * I) / L, where μ₀ is the permeability of free space, N is the number of turns, I is the current, and L is the inductance. Plugging in the values, we calculate:B = (4π x 10-7 H/m * 100 * 2 A) / (1 x 10-3 H) = 0.022 Wbm-2.

questions.text: For the solenoids shown in the diagram (which are assumed to be close to each other), the resistance of the left-hand circuit is slowly increased. In which direction does the current flow through the galvanometer in the right-hand circuit?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Increasing the resistance causes a decrease in the current on the left. This reduces the field strength for the solenoid B=µ0nl. By the right-hand rule, the field through the solenoid is directed to the RIGHT. Hence, in the right-hand circuit, since there will be fewer field lines directed to the right, there is an induced electric field that will produce a current in the wires to "replace" the lost field lines. This means that the current will be directed with the same orientation as the current in the left circuit and the galvanometer will deflect to the LEFT.
It will be correct only in case if the resistance of the circuit is slowly decreasing.
In this scenario current is present.So this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.andMagnetic field is created whenever an electric current flows through a conductor. This is because a moving electrical charge creates a magnetic field. The strength and direction of the magnetic field depend on the amount of current flowing and the direction of flow. Magnetic fields can also be created by permanent magnets (bar magnets). These are made from materials that are naturally good conductors of electricity, such as iron. The molecules in these materials line up in rows, which creates a strong magnetic field. When you bring a conductor close to a magnet, the magnetic field from the magnet affects the moving charges in the conductor. This causes current flow, creating a current-carrying conductor, which creates its own magnetic field. These two fields interact with each other, resulting in a force that either attracts or repels the objects.

questions.text: A cyclotron operates with a given magnetic field and at a given frequency. If "R" denotes the radius of the final orbit, the energy of particle is proportional to:

questions.explanation: In a cyclotron, charged particles (such as electrons or ions) are accelerated by a magnetic field and an oscillating electric field. The key principle in a cyclotron is that themagnetic field causes the charged particles to move in a circular path, and the oscillating electric field alternates the polarity of the accelerating voltage at just the right frequency to keep the particles accelerating. The kinetic energy of a charged particle in a cyclotron can be expressed as: K.E. = (1/2)mv^2 In a cyclotron, the magnetic field (B) and the frequency of the oscillating electric field (f) are kept constant. As the particle is accelerated, its velocity increases, and its kinetic energy also increases. The radius (R) of the circular path is related to the velocity and the magnetic field strength by the equation: mv = qBR Solving for v, we get: v = (qBR) / m Now, we can substitute this expression for v into the kinetic energy equation: K.E. = (1/2)m[(qBR) / m]^2 K.E. is proportional to (BR)^2. So, in a cyclotron, the kinetic energy of the particle is proportional to the square of the product of the magnetic field strength (B) and the radius (R) of the circular path.

questions.explanation: When a charged particle enters a region where both electric and magnetic fields are parallel and oriented in the same direction, the force experienced by the charge is a combination of both the electric and magnetic forces acting on it.The force on a charged particle in an electric field (E) is given by:F_electric = q * Ewhere q is the charge of the particle.The force on a charged particle moving with velocity (v) in a magnetic field (B) is given by:F_magnetic = q * v * BIn this case, since both fields are parallel and oriented in the same direction, the velocity of the particle and the direction of the magnetic force are the same. questions.text: A proton and an alpha particle, both are moving with same initial velocity, perpendicular to uniform magnetic field of 1 T. Find the ratio of radii of circles described by the alpha particle and the proton.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The radius of the circular path is proportional to the mass-to-charge ratio. The alpha particle has twice the charge and about four times the mass of a proton, resulting in a radius that is twice that of the proton.

This would imply that the radius of the alpha particle's path is half that of the proton's, which is incorrect because the alpha particle's larger mass increases the radius, despite its larger charge.
If the radii were equal, it would mean the mass-to-charge ratio of the proton and alpha particle is the same, which is not the case.
This would suggest the alpha particle's path radius is four times that of the proton's, which overestimates the effect of the mass increase relative to the increase in charge.

questions.explanation: The radius of a charged particle's path in a magnetic field is given by the formula r = (mv) / (qB), where m is the mass, v is the velocity, q is the charge, and B is the magnetic field strength. For a proton, m = m_p, q = e. For an alpha particle, m = 4m_p, q = 2e. Given the same velocity and magnetic field, the radius for the proton is r_p = (m_p * v) / (e * B) and for the alpha particle is r_alpha = (4m_p * v) / (2e * B). Simplifying, r_alpha / r_p = 2. questions.explanation: The force on a charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field is zero.When a charged particle moves parallel to the magnetic field lines (i.e., the velocity vector is parallel to the magnetic field vector), the angle (θ) between the velocity vector (v) and the magnetic field vector (B) is 0 degrees. questions.text: Ampere's law is applicable to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ampere's law is a fundamental principle in electromagnetism and is applicable to any closed path. It states that the line integral of the magnetic field (B) around a closed path (also known as a closed loop) is equal to the product of the total current passing through the enclosed area and the permeability of the medium.Mathematically, Ampere's law can be expressed as:∮ B · dl = μ₀ * Σ IWhere:∮ B · dl represents the line integral of the magnetic field B around the closed path.μ₀ (mu naught) is the permeability of free space, approximately 4π x 10^-7 T·m/A.Σ I represents the algebraic sum of the currents passing through the enclosed area by the closed path.The closed path can be of any shape, such as a circle, rectangle, square, or any arbitrary closed curve. As long as the path forms a closed loop and encloses an area, Ampere's law is applicable. It provides a powerful tool for calculating magnetic fields in various scenarios, especially in cases where there is symmetry in the current distribution.

This option is correct. In a step-up transformer, the turn ratio (N) is defined as the ratio of the number of turns in the secondary coil (Ns) to the number of turns in the primary coil (Np). If the turn ratio is given as 50 (N = Ns/Np = 50), we can determine the voltage and current ratios. Voltage Ratio:The voltage ratio (Vr) of a transformer is given by the turn ratio (N). In a step-up transformer, the voltage ratio is greater than 1, indicating that the secondary voltage (Vs) is greater than the primary voltage (Vp). Vr = Vs/Vp = N Since the turn ratio (N) is 50, the voltage ratio will also be 50. Current Ratio:The current ratio (Ir) of a transformer is inversely proportional to the voltage ratio (Vr). This is due to the principle of conservation of power. Ir = Ip/Is = 1/Vr Substituting the value of voltage ratio (Vr = 50), we can calculate the current ratio. Ir = 1/50 Therefore, in a step-up transformer with a turn ratio of 50, the voltage ratio will be 50, and the current ratio will be 1/50 = 0.02

options.explanation: If the magnetic field were increasing into the page, the induced current would be counterclockwise due to Lenz's law. However, in this scenario, the magnetic field is decreasing into the page, leading to a clockwise induced current.

questions.text: For a good transformer, the material of the core should have the hysteresis loop of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The core of the transformer should be assembled from the laminated sheets of a material whose hysteresis loop area is very small. This is because a small hysteresis loop area indicates lower hysteresis losses in the core material. Hysteresis losses occur due to the repeated magnetization and demagnetization cycles of the core material as the alternating current passes through the transformer. The energy dissipated as heat during these cycles leads to inefficiencies in the transformer. By minimizing hysteresis losses through the use of a core material with a small hysteresis loop area, the transformer can operate more efficiently, leading to reduced energy wastage and improved overall performance.
A hysteresis loop of a large area denotes that there are large hysteresis losses, which makes the transformer less efficient.

questions.text: A copper ring is held horizontally and a bar magnet is dropped through the ring with its length along the axis of the ring. The acceleration of the falling magnet is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Equal to that due to gravity
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g"

options.text: Less than that due to gravity
options.explanation: When a bar magnet is dropped through the ring, according to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current will decrease (oppose) the motion of the freely falling bar magnet. So, the acceleration of the bar magnet is less than "g".

options.text: More than that due to gravity
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g"

options.text: Depends on the diameter of the ring and the length of the magnet
options.explanation: The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than "g", and does not depend on the diameter of the ring and the length of the magnet.

questions.explanation: The explanation is: When a bar magnet is dropped through a copper ring with its length along the axis of the ring, it induces eddy currents in the ring. These eddy currents create a magnetic field that opposes the motion of the falling magnet due to Lenz's law. As a result, the falling magnet experiences a magnetic damping force. This magnetic damping force acts against the force of gravity, and it effectively reduces the acceleration of the falling magnet. The magnet is not in free fall because it is experiencing this additional magnetic force, making its acceleration less than that due to gravity. So, option b is the correct answer. The acceleration of the falling magnet is less than that due to gravity due to the opposing magnetic force generated by the ring.

This option is correct. To determine the number of turns in the secondary coil of the transformer, we can use the turns ratio formula, which relates the primary and secondary voltages and the number of turns in each coil. The formula is as follows: Turns ratio (Np/Ns) = Voltage ratio (Vp/Vs) Given information: Primary voltage (Vp) = 220 V Secondary voltage (Vs) = 4.4 kV = 4400 V Number of turns in the primary coil (Np) = 100 Using the turns ratio formula: Np/Ns = Vp/Vs Substituting the known values: 100/Ns = 220/4400 Simplifying the equation: 100/Ns = 1/20 Cross-multiplying: Ns = 100 * 20 Ns = 2000 Therefore, the number of turns in the secondary coil is 2,000.

The relationship between the voltages and currents is given by the equation: Vp / Vs = Np / Ns = Is / Ip, where V is voltage, I is current, and N is the number of turns in the primary and secondary coils.Given that it's a 1:8 step-down transformer, the secondary current is 25 A, and the primary power is 10 kW. First, we find the secondary voltage using the power equation: P = V * I, thus Vs = P / Is = 10,000 W / 25 A = 400 V. questions.text: Purpose of step down transformer is to make the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Input current same as output current
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The primary purpose of a step-down transformer is not to make the input current the same as the output current. Transformers primarily deal with voltage transformations.

options.text: Output voltage lower than input voltage
options.explanation: This is correct. A step-down transformer is designed to reduce the output voltage compared to the input voltage. It has fewer turns in the secondary coil than in the primary coil, which lowers the output voltage.

options.text: Output current higher than input current
options.explanation: This is incorrect. A transformer doesn't inherently increase current; it can only change the voltage. The output current depends on the load connected to the transformer and is typically adjusted to satisfy the law of conservation of power.

options.text: Output current lower than input current
options.explanation: "Output current lower than input current" is incorrect. In a transformer, the relationship between input and output current is inversely proportional to the voltage. When the voltage is stepped down (reduced) from the input to the output, the current is stepped up (increased) to maintain the conservation of power, as described by the equation P = VI. Therefore, the output current is generally higher than the input current in a step-down transformer.

questions.explanation: A transformer in which voltage across secondary is less than the primary is called a step down transformer.

questions.text: A magnet is introduced into the coil and a voltage is induced across the coil. Which of the following factor has no effect on the induced votage?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Thickness of the wire of the coil
options.explanation: The thickness (diameter) of the wire in a coil can have an effect on the induced voltage, although it might not be a primary factor. The induced voltage in a coil is mainly determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors like the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnetic field change, the number of turns in the coil, and the orientation of the coil. The thickness of the wire affects the coil's resistance and self-inductance. Thicker wires generally have lower resistance, which means that the induced current due to the induced voltage might be larger in a coil with thicker wire. Additionally, the thickness of the wire could influence the coil's self-inductance, affecting how the coil reacts to changes in current. However, the thickness of the wire is not usually a primary factor in determining the induced voltage compared to factors like the rate of change of magnetic flux or the strength of the magnetic field.

options.text: The time in which magnet is introduced
options.explanation: The time in which a magnet is introduced into a coil does not affect the induced voltage. The induced voltage in a coil is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors such as the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnet's movement, and the number of turns in the coil. The specific time at which the magnet is introduced does not directly impact the induced voltage, as long as the rate of change of magnetic flux remains the same. The induced voltage is a result of how quickly the magnetic flux changes, not the exact moment at which the magnet is introduced.

options.text: The strength of the magnetic field
options.explanation: The strength of the magnetic field does have an effect on the induced voltage in a coil. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is affected by the strength of the magnetic field, among other factors. If the strength of the magnetic field increases, assuming other factors remain constant, the rate of change of magnetic flux will increase as well. This, in turn, will lead to a higher induced voltage. So, the strength of the magnetic field does indeed affect the induced voltage in a coil.

options.text: Number of turns of the coil
options.explanation: The number of turns in the coil does indeed have an effect on the induced voltage. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is influenced by factors including the strength of the magnetic field and the number of turns in the coil. Specifically, if the number of turns in the coil increases, the induced voltage will also increase for the same rate of change of magnetic flux. This is because each turn of the coil contributes to the overall flux linkage, leading to a higher induced voltage. So, the number of turns of the coil does have an effect on the induced voltage.

questions.text: One can increase the performance.of a transformer by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Using primary and secondary wires of less resistance
options.explanation: This is correct. Lower resistance in the primary and secondary windings of a transformer reduces energy losses due to heat (I^2R losses), making the transformer more efficient. Lower resistance means that a larger portion of the input power is efficiently transferred to the output.

options.text: Making the sheets of transformer core fully insulated
options.explanation: This option is correct, but it is more related to minimizing losses in the transformer rather than directly increasing its performance. Proper insulation of the core's sheets reduces eddy current losses and helps prevent energy loss due to heating. While it doesn't directly boost the transformer's efficiency or power transfer capability, it does contribute to better performance by ensuring that a larger portion of the input power is efficiently transferred to the output without being lost as heat.

options.text: Using methods to increase the flux coupling between the coils
options.explanation: This is correct. Maximizing the magnetic coupling between the primary and secondary coils is essential for efficient energy transfer. Methods like using a well-designed core, closely winding the coils, and minimizing air gaps between them all improve the flux coupling and, therefore, enhance the transformer's performance.

Option "d" is correct because using primary and secondary wires of less resistance and employing methods to increase the flux coupling between the coils are both effective ways to improve a transformer's performance.

questions.explanation: In order to improve the efficiency, to minimize all the power losses: Core should be assembled from the laminated sheets of a material whose hysteresis loop area is very small. The insulation between lamination sheets should be perfect so as to stop the flow of eddy currents. The resistance of primary and secondary coils should be kept to minimum. As power transfer from primary to secondary takes place through flux linkages, so the primary and secondary coils should be wound in such a way that flux coupling between them is maximum.

questions.text: According to Lenz's law, the direction of which of the following is always so as to oppose the change which causes it?

options.explanation: Lenz's law doesn't refer to the direction of the induced emf itself but rather to the direction of the induced current. This is correct. Lenz's law states that the direction of the induced current in a conductor is always such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that caused it. This opposition is due to the creation of a magnetic field by the induced current, which acts to counteract the change in magnetic flux.

Magnetic flux is not a quantity with a direction in itself, but it depends on the area, magnetic field, and the angle between them. Lenz's law is concerned with the direction of the induced current, not magnetic flux.

Magnetic induction typically refers to the process of inducing a magnetic field in a material due to the influence of an external magnetic field. It doesn't have a direction of its own; it's more of a property of a material. Lenz's law primarily deals with the direction of the induced current in response to changes in magnetic flux, not the direction of magnetic induction.

Option "d" (Ns/Np = Ip/Is) is correct because it accurately represents the relationship between the turns ratio and the current ratio in a transformer. The turns ratio (Ns/Np) indicates how many times the secondary voltage differs from the primary voltage, and it is proportional to the current ratio (Ip/Is). This means that when the number of turns in the secondary coil (Ns) is greater than in the primary coil (Np), it's a step-up transformer, and the secondary current (Is) is smaller than the primary current (Ip). Conversely, for a step-down transformer, where Ns is less than Np, the secondary current (Is) is greater than the primary current (Ip). This relationship is a fundamental aspect of transformer operation.

questions.text: Which quantity is increased in step down transformer?

options.explanation: A step-down transformer, the current is increased on the secondary side compared to the primary side. This is because of the conservation of power; when voltage decreases (step down), current increases to maintain the same power (P = VI). So, this option is correct.
Power is not directly increased or decreased in a transformer. It remains constant (neglecting losses) due to the law of conservation of energy.
In a step-down transformer, the voltage is decreased on the secondary side compared to the primary side. This is the primary purpose of a step-down transformer, to reduce voltage.
Transformers do not change the frequency of the AC power they handle. The frequency remains the same on both the primary and secondary sides.

questions.text: The S.I unit of magnetic flux density is:

questions.explanation: The S.I unit of magnetic flux density is the Tesla (T). Magnetic flux density, often represented by the symbol B, is defined as the amount of magnetic flux passing through a unit area perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field. The Tesla is equivalent to one Weber per square meter (Wb/m2). Option B (Weber) is incorrect because it measures magnetic flux, not flux density. Option C (Ampere per meter) is incorrect as it measures magnetic field strength (H). Option D (Gauss) is a unit in the CGS system, not in the S.I system.

questions.text: The soft iron core is used in a transformer in order to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It helps to efficiently guide and increase the magnetic flux generated by the primary winding, allowing for effective induction of voltage in the secondary winding. This is key in the transformer's function of transferring electrical energy from one circuit to another. This is the primary purpose of the soft iron core in a transformer. It facilitates the efficient generation and transfer of magnetic flux, crucial for inducing a voltage in the secondary winding.
While the soft iron core does add some weight to the transformer, its primary function is not to increase weight but to facilitate the magnetic flux.

The presence of a soft iron core doesn't directly lead to an increase in copper loss. Copper loss in transformers typically occurs due to resistance in the conductive wires (copper windings), not due to the core material.

The soft iron core doesn't specifically influence the copper loss. Copper loss is mitigated by using better conductive materials or reducing resistance in the copper windings, not by the core material.

questions.text: The torque in the coil can be increased by increasing:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Increasing the number of turns in the coil will result in a stronger magnetic interaction with the external magnetic field. This leads to an increase in the torque experienced by the coil.
A larger coil will intercept more magnetic field lines, resulting in a greater magnetic flux passing through the coil. As a result, the torque on the coil will increase.

The torque experienced by the coil is directly proportional to the product of the current passing through the coil and the strength of the external magnetic field. Increasing either the current through the coil or the strength of the magnetic field (or both) will result in a larger torque on the coil.

The torque experienced by a coil in a magnetic field can be increased by increasing any or all of the following factors: a) Number of turns: Increasing the number of turns in the coil will result in a stronger magnetic interaction with the external magnetic field. This leads to an increase in the torque experienced by the coil. b) Area of the coil: A larger coil will intercept more magnetic field lines, resulting in a greater magnetic flux passing through the coil. As a result, the torque on the coil will increase. c) Current and magnetic field: The torque experienced by the coil is directly proportional to the product of the current passing through the coil and the strength of the external magnetic field. Increasing either the current through the coil or the strength of the magnetic field (or both) will result in a larger torque on the coil. In summary, all of the factors mentioned (number of turns, area of the coil, and current/magnetic field) contribute to increasing the torque experienced by the coil in a magnetic field.
